title: Lumber and Plywood Storage Cart
summary: A rolling 2x4-frame cart with plywood-floored vertical bins for storing full sheets on edge, plus a flat top shelf and angled scrap-wood bins.
description:
Storing full sheets of plywood on edge, rather than flat, is the entire point of this cart — flat-stacked sheets are heavy to dig through and warp under their own weight over time, while vertical slots let you pull one sheet without disturbing the rest. Add the caster wheels right after the base is built, while the frame is still light and easy to flip — the source is direct about this, since waiting until the walls and floor are on makes wheel access awkward.

The scrap-wood bins are a genuinely simple sub-build: four identical side/divider pieces cut with an angled top from a single plywood strip, then a front and back attached across them. That angled top is what lets you see and grab an offcut from above without the bin walls blocking the view — a square-topped bin would hide everything below the rim.

tools.note: Cuts the 45° angled cross-support and scrap-bin ends.

materials.name: Plywood or 1x material, 3/4", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Scrap wood bins and top shelf.

cutList.note: Both ends cut at 45°, not parallel, long point to long point measurement.
Tapered to 12" on one side at a 45° angle.

steps.title: Cut the frame and panel parts
steps.body: From the 8 ft 2x4s cut two base long rails and four wall top/bottom rails at 96" — six boards used at their full length — then five base cross members at 45", two to a board. From the 10 ft 2x4s cut ten wall verticals at 60", two per board; a 10 ft board is 120" and two 60" cuts leave nothing for the saw kerf, so measure your stock before committing and buy a sixth board if any of them come up short. From the 8 ft 1x4s cut two top trim long at 96" (a full board each), four cross supports at 25-1/2" with both ends at 45° and NOT parallel — that is the long-point-to-long-point measurement — and two top trim short at 20-1/2"; the 1x4 works out to exactly four boards with nothing spare. Rip the 1/2" plywood into 96"-long strips: two floor panels 14-1/2" wide, one floor panel 12" wide, and the two scrap-bin strips at 36" and 12" wide. From the 3/4" sheet take the 19" x 96" top panel and four scrap bin sides/dividers 13" x 25", each tapered to 12" on one side at 45°.

steps.title: Build and mount the base
steps.body: Lay the two 96" base long rails parallel and set the five 45" cross members between them, one at each end and three spaced along the length — the long rails run the full 96" and the cross members fit between, so the finished base is 96" long and 48" wide overall (45" plus 1-1/2" of rail). Screw through the outside of the long rails into the end grain of each cross member with 2-1/2" screws, or use pocket holes at the 1-1/2" setting with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws. Check the diagonals match before the last screws go in; this frame sets the geometry for everything above it. Then flip the base and mount the four heavy-duty casters at the corners NOW, while the frame is still light enough for one person to turn over — once the walls and floor panels are on, getting underneath is genuinely awkward.

steps.title: Build and attach the walls
steps.body: Build two identical wall frames flat on the floor, each from five 60" wall verticals with a 96" rail at the top and the bottom, verticals at both ends and evenly spaced between. Stand them on the base and position them so the three floor bays come out 14-1/2", 14-1/2" and 12" wide — those three bays plus the two 3-1/2"-wide wall frames add up to the base's 48" width exactly, so the wall positions are fixed by the floor panels, not free to eyeball. Screw down through each wall's bottom rail into the base framing where you can reach, and toe-screw at an angle through the wall verticals into the base where the rail is in the way. Check both walls are plumb before driving the last screws: a full sheet leaning in a racked bay will only push it further over.

steps.title: Add the floor panels
steps.body: Drop the plywood floor panels into the three bays — the two 14-1/2" x 96" panels into the outer bays and the 12" x 96" panel into the narrow one — and screw them down into the base cross members with 1-1/2" screws. There are five cross members, so put a screw into each one, five per panel; a sheet of plywood standing on edge concentrates a lot of weight on a small footprint and an unsupported span will dish. These floors are also what keeps sheet goods up off the shop floor and out of anything tracked in.

steps.title: Add the cross bracing
steps.body: Set the four 25-1/2" cross supports diagonally into the wall frames, corner to corner between a vertical and a rail — their 45° ends are cut long point to long point, so each one seats tight into the corner it was cut for. Screw through the face of each brace into the framing with 1-1/2" screws, two at each end. A diagonal is what actually stops a tall frame racking; without one the whole cart leans in whichever direction you last pushed it, and a leaning rack loads its casters unevenly.

steps.title: Add the top shelf
steps.body: Set the 19" x 96" top panel across the wall frames, then frame it with the 1x4 trim: the two 20-1/2" short pieces go at the ends and run the full width, and the two 96" long pieces fit between them front and back. That is what makes the trim close — the panel and the long trim are both 96", so both tuck between the 3/4"-thick end pieces, and the panel's 19" width plus 3/4" of trim front and back gives the 20-1/2" ends exactly. Screw the trim on with 1-1/2" screws so it stands slightly proud of the panel surface; that small lip is what keeps offcuts from sliding off the top when you roll the cart.

steps.title: Build the scrap wood bins
steps.body: Cut the four scrap bin sides/dividers 13" x 25" and taper each to 12" on one side at 45°. Cut all four from the same strip, stacked or gang-marked, so they are genuinely identical — that angled top is what lets you look down into the bin and see the offcuts, where a square rim hides everything below it. Stand them up as the two outer sides plus two dividers and run the plywood scrap-bin strips across them as the front and back, screwing through the strips into the edge of each piece with 1-1/2" screws. Space the two inner dividers to suit the offcuts you actually keep — they only need to be parallel to each other, not evenly spaced — and fasten the finished bin assembly to the cart so it can't tip when you dig through it.

images.alt: Rolling lumber and plywood storage cart with scrap wood bins
